title_en: Ibn Sa'd: Secretary to the Prophet's Biographer
title_id: Ibn Sa'd: Sekretaris Penulis Biografi Nabi
content_id:
Muhammad ibn Sa'd ibn Mani' al-Hashimi al-Basri al-Baghdadi, yang dikenal sederhana sebagai Ibn Sa'd (168–230 H / 784–845 M), adalah salah satu tokoh fundamental dalam literatur biografi Islam. Lahir di Basra, ia menghabiskan sebagian besar karier keilmuannya di Baghdad dan menjadi sekretaris (katib) Muhammad ibn Umar al-Waqidi, penulis biografi Nabi yang terkenal di masa awal. Hubungan ini memberi Ibn Sa'd akses terhadap penelitian ekstensif al-Waqidi tentang sejarah Islam awal sekaligus menjadikan metode keilmuan al-Waqidi sebagai model.

Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra karya Ibn Sa'd — Kamus Biografi Besar, atau lebih harfiah, Kitab Generasi Utama — adalah karya biografi Islam sistematis berskala besar yang paling awal yang masih bertahan. Judulnya merujuk pada konsep tabaqat (generasi atau kelompok): karya ini diorganisir di sekitar generasi-generasi Muslim yang berurutan, dari para Sahabat Nabi melalui para penerus dan ke generasi-generasi berikutnya hingga zaman Ibn Sa'd sendiri.

Skala dan ambisi karya ini luar biasa. Dalam edisi modern lengkapnya, karya ini mencakup lima belas jilid dan memuat biografi lebih dari empat ribu individu. Cakupannya dimulai dengan biografi terperinci tentang Nabi Muhammad sendiri, dilanjutkan dengan biografi para Sahabat yang diorganisir berdasarkan keislaman awal atau kemudian, partisipasi mereka dalam peperangan, dan suku-suku mereka, kemudian dilanjutkan melalui para penerus dan generasi-generasi berikutnya.

Karya ini disusun pada momen kritis dalam sejarah intelektual Islam: abad kedua Hijriah, ketika ingatan hidup dari generasi paling awal baru saja berlalu dan kebutuhan mendesak untuk mencatat dan melestarikan apa yang masih bisa dikumpulkan dirasakan secara mendalam. Ibn Sa'd dan para sezamannya — tokoh-tokoh seperti Ahmad ibn Hanbal dan Yahya ibn Ma'in dalam bidang hadis, dan al-Waqidi dalam biografi sejarah — terlibat dalam upaya kolektif yang masif untuk mendokumentasikan warisan keilmuan Islam sebelum hilang.

Metode Ibn Sa'd menggabungkan informasi dari berbagai sumber: wawancaranya sendiri dengan para ulama yang telah meriwayatkan laporan dari generasi-generasi awal, catatan penelitian ekstensif al-Waqidi, dan sumber-sumber tertulis yang tersedia baginya. Hasilnya adalah karya dengan kekayaan luar biasa yang melestarikan informasi yang tidak tersedia dari sumber lain manapun.
content_en:
Muhammad ibn Sa'd ibn Mani' al-Hashimi al-Basri al-Baghdadi, known simply as Ibn Sa'd (168–230 AH / 784–845 CE), was one of the foundational figures of Islamic biographical literature. Born in Basra, he spent much of his scholarly career in Baghdad and became the secretary (katib) of Muhammad ibn Umar al-Waqidi, the famous early biographer of the Prophet. This relationship gave Ibn Sa'd access to al-Waqidi's extensive research on early Islamic history while also serving al-Waqidi's scholarly methods as a model.

Ibn Sa'd's Al-Tabaqat al-Kubra — The Large Biographical Dictionary, or more literally, The Major Book of Generations — is the earliest surviving large-scale systematic work of Islamic biography. The title refers to the concept of tabaqat (generations or classes): the work is organized around successive generations of Muslims, from the Prophet's companions through the successors and into subsequent generations down to Ibn Sa'd's own time.

The scale and ambition of the work are remarkable. In its complete modern edition, it runs to fifteen volumes and contains biographies of more than four thousand individuals. The coverage begins with a detailed biography of the Prophet Muhammad himself, continues through biographies of the companions organized by their early or late conversion, their participation in the battles, and their tribes, and then proceeds through the successors and later generations.

The work was composed at a critical moment in Islamic intellectual history: the second century AH, when the living memory of the earliest generation was just passing away and the urgent need to record and preserve what could still be gathered was acutely felt. Ibn Sa'd and his contemporaries — figures like Ahmad ibn Hanbal and Yahya ibn Ma'in in hadith, and al-Waqidi in historical biography — were engaged in a massive collective effort to document the Islamic scholarly heritage before it was lost.

Ibn Sa'd's method combined information from multiple sources: his own interviews with scholars who had transmitted reports from early generations, the extensive research notes of al-Waqidi, and the written sources available to him. The result is a work of extraordinary richness that preserves information available from no other source.
